questions.prompt:
Read the passage and answer the question.
The City’s Sound Map
When the city of Marston began receiving complaints about noise, officials first assumed the problem was mostly about late-night music and construction. But the health department wanted better information before recommending changes. With help from a local university, the city placed small sound sensors on light poles in twelve neighborhoods for six weeks. The sensors did not record conversations. They measured only sound levels and times of day.
The results showed patterns that residents had described for years. In some areas, the loudest periods were not weekend nights but early mornings, when delivery trucks idled near apartment buildings. In another neighborhood, the highest sound levels occurred near a highway ramp where drivers accelerated after a traffic light. A third area had short bursts of noise from a metal recycling yard, especially when trucks unloaded before 7:00 a.m.
The sound map did not lead to one simple solution. Instead, it helped the city match problems with specific responses. Delivery companies were asked to turn off engines when parked. Traffic engineers adjusted the timing of one signal near the highway ramp. The recycling yard agreed to delay outdoor unloading until later in the morning.
City officials emphasized that the goal was not silence. A city includes buses, businesses, children, and emergency vehicles. The goal was to reduce avoidable noise in places where it regularly disturbed sleep, work, and daily life. By measuring the problem carefully, Marston moved from general complaints to practical changes.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Marston used sound data to identify specific noise problems and respond with targeted solutions.
choices.B: Marston banned all delivery trucks, recycling yards, and highway ramps.
choices.C: The sound sensors recorded private conversations throughout the city.
choices.D: Residents stopped caring about noise after the study ended.

questions.correctAnswerText: Marston used sound data to identify specific noise problems and respond with targeted solutions.
questions.explanation: The passage explains how the city measured noise patterns and used the results to make specific changes.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The City’s Sound Map.”
Why did the health department use sound sensors?

choices.A: To prove that residents were exaggerating all noise complaints
choices.B: To gather better information before recommending changes
choices.C: To record conversations near apartment buildings
choices.D: To replace traffic engineers with university students

questions.correctAnswerText: To gather better information before recommending changes
questions.explanation: The health department wanted reliable information before deciding what changes to recommend.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “The City’s Sound Map.”
Which TWO noise sources were identified in the study?

choices.A: Delivery trucks idling near apartment buildings
choices.B: A metal recycling yard unloading trucks before 7:00 a.m.
choices.C: Fireworks at a city festival every weekend
choices.D: School announcements played over outdoor speakers
choices.E: Music from a new concert hall downtown

questions.correctAnswerText: Delivery trucks idling near apartment buildings | A metal recycling yard unloading trucks before 7:00 a.m.
questions.explanation: Both sources are specifically identified in the sound map results.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The City’s Sound Map.”
What can be inferred from the city’s different responses to different neighborhoods?

choices.A: The city recognized that noise problems can have different causes and require different solutions.
choices.B: The city decided that all neighborhoods should follow the same rule regardless of the source of noise.
choices.C: The city concluded that noise was not a serious concern anywhere.
choices.D: The city asked residents to stop reporting noise problems.

questions.correctAnswerText: The city recognized that noise problems can have different causes and require different solutions.
questions.explanation: The city used different responses because the noise sources varied by neighborhood.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The City’s Sound Map.”
In the sentence “By measuring the problem carefully, Marston moved from general complaints to practical changes,” practical most nearly means [D1].
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=useful and realistic
questions.explanation: In context, practical means useful and realistic rather than theoretical.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The City’s Sound Map.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Sensors measured sound levels and times of day without recording conversations.
tiles.B: The recycling yard agreed to delay outdoor unloading.
tiles.C: City officials said the goal was not silence.
tiles.D: The university asked residents to stop sleeping near windows.

targets.T2: Evidence of a specific response to a noise source
targets.T3: Evidence that the city had a balanced goal

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Sensors measured sound levels and times of day without recording conversations.; T2=The recycling yard agreed to delay outdoor unloading.; T3=City officials said the goal was not silence.
questions.explanation: The tiles match privacy, a targeted response, and the city’s balanced goal.

questions.prompt:
Read the workplace document and answer the question.
Inventory Count Correction Form
To: Stockroom, Sales Floor, and Receiving Staff
From: Store Operations Manager
Subject: Inventory Count Correction Form
Beginning September 10, employees must use the Inventory Count Correction Form whenever the number of items on the shelf or in the stockroom does not match the number shown in the inventory system. The form will help managers determine whether the difference is caused by a receiving error, misplaced merchandise, damaged items, theft, or a scanning mistake.
Before submitting the form, employees should check nearby shelves, return carts, and the damaged-goods bin. If the item is found, update its location in the system instead of submitting a correction form. Do not change the inventory count without approval from a department lead.
The form requires the item number, item description, current system count, actual count, location checked, employee name, and date. If the item was damaged, attach the damaged-goods tag. If the item arrived in the most recent delivery, include the receiving label number.
This procedure is not intended to blame employees for every count difference. Accurate inventory helps customers know whether an item is available and helps buyers avoid ordering too much or too little. Questions about the form should be directed to a department lead before the end of the shift.
What is the main purpose of the document?

choices.A: To explain when and how employees should report inventory count differences
choices.B: To announce that employees may change inventory numbers without approval
choices.C: To require customers to count merchandise before buying it
choices.D: To cancel all future deliveries to the store

questions.correctAnswerText: To explain when and how employees should report inventory count differences
questions.explanation: The document explains the new correction form and the steps employees must follow.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Inventory Count Correction Form.”
What should employees do before submitting a correction form?

choices.A: Check nearby shelves, return carts, and the damaged-goods bin.
choices.B: Delete the item from the inventory system.
choices.C: Call every customer who bought the item.
choices.D: Submit the form without looking for the missing item.

questions.correctAnswerText: Check nearby shelves, return carts, and the damaged-goods bin.
questions.explanation: Employees are instructed to check these locations before submitting a correction form.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Inventory Count Correction Form.”
Which TWO pieces of information are required on the form?

choices.A: The item number
choices.B: The actual count
choices.C: The customer’s home address
choices.D: The employee’s preferred schedule

questions.correctAnswerText: The item number | The actual count
questions.explanation: The form requires both the item number and the actual count, along with other information.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Inventory Count Correction Form.”
Why should employees attach the damaged-goods tag when an item was damaged?

choices.A: It helps explain why the inventory count may differ.
choices.B: It allows the employee to skip department lead approval.
choices.C: It proves that the item was sold to a customer.
choices.D: It replaces the need to include the item number.

questions.correctAnswerText: It helps explain why the inventory count may differ.
questions.explanation: A damaged-goods tag provides evidence that damage may have caused the count difference.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Inventory Count Correction Form.”
Which sentence best explains why accurate inventory matters?

choices.A: “Beginning September 10, employees must use the Inventory Count Correction Form.”
choices.B: “Before submitting the form, employees should check nearby shelves, return carts, and the damaged-goods bin.”
choices.C: “Accurate inventory helps customers know whether an item is available and helps buyers avoid ordering too much or too little.”
choices.D: “Questions about the form should be directed to a department lead before the end of the shift.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Accurate inventory helps customers know whether an item is available and helps buyers avoid ordering too much or too little.”
questions.explanation: This sentence explains the practical reason for the procedure.

questions.prompt:
Read the argument and answer the question.
Support Skill-Building Microgrants
The city should offer small skill-building microgrants to adults who want short, job-related training. A microgrant of $300 or $500 will not pay for a full degree, but it can cover a certification test fee, a required work uniform, a childcare payment during a weekend class, or transportation to a training site. For adults living close to the edge financially, these smaller costs often decide whether training is possible.
The program should be simple but responsible. Applicants could submit proof of enrollment in an approved course, a short statement of career goals, and a receipt after the money is used. The city could limit each person to one grant per year and review outcomes after six months. The point is to remove practical barriers, not to hand out money without purpose.
Some critics may argue that workforce programs should focus only on large scholarships. Large scholarships are valuable, but they do not solve every problem. A parent who has already found a low-cost nursing assistant course may still miss the opportunity if the exam fee is due before payday. A warehouse worker offered forklift training may need help paying for transportation during the training week.
A microgrant program would not replace education funding, apprenticeships, or career counseling. It would fill a gap that larger programs often miss. Small amounts of money, used carefully, can help motivated adults take the next step toward better work.
What is the author’s main claim?

choices.A: The city should offer small microgrants because minor costs can prevent adults from completing useful training.
choices.B: The city should stop funding all large scholarships and degree programs.
choices.C: Adults should avoid training unless it is completely free.
choices.D: Employers should never ask workers to complete certification tests.

questions.correctAnswerText: The city should offer small microgrants because minor costs can prevent adults from completing useful training.
questions.explanation: The author argues that small financial barriers can block adults from training opportunities.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Support Skill-Building Microgrants.”
Which evidence best supports the claim that small costs can block training?

choices.A: A certification test fee or transportation cost may determine whether an adult can attend training.
choices.B: The city can limit each person to one grant per year.
choices.C: Large scholarships are valuable.
choices.D: Applicants could submit a short statement of career goals.

questions.correctAnswerText: A certification test fee or transportation cost may determine whether an adult can attend training.
questions.explanation: This evidence directly supports the claim that small costs can prevent training.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to “Support Skill-Building Microgrants.”

choices.A: Requiring proof of enrollment in an approved course
choices.B: Limiting each person to one grant per year
choices.C: Giving money without receipts or follow-up
choices.D: Requiring applicants to quit their current jobs
choices.E: Allowing only college professors to apply

questions.correctAnswerText: Requiring proof of enrollment in an approved course | Limiting each person to one grant per year
questions.explanation: Both are safeguards the author proposes for responsible use of microgrants.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Support Skill-Building Microgrants.”
How does the author respond to the idea that workforce programs should focus only on large scholarships?

choices.A: By arguing that large scholarships are useful but do not solve smaller practical barriers
choices.B: By saying that large scholarships should be illegal
choices.C: By claiming that adults never need transportation or childcare
choices.D: By suggesting that all training programs should close

questions.correctAnswerText: By arguing that large scholarships are useful but do not solve smaller practical barriers
questions.explanation: The author acknowledges the value of scholarships while explaining why microgrants still fill a gap.

questions.prompt:

questions.correct: D1=A local nonprofit reported that nearly one-third of adults who left short training programs cited transportation, fees, or childcare as the main reason.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=A local nonprofit reported that nearly one-third of adults who left short training programs cited transportation, fees, or childcare as the main reason.
questions.explanation: This evidence strengthens the argument by showing that small financial barriers affect program completion.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Support Skill-Building Microgrants.”
Which assumption is most important to the author’s argument?

choices.A: Removing small financial barriers can help some adults complete training they are already motivated to pursue.
choices.B: Every adult who receives a grant will immediately become wealthy.
choices.C: Career counseling is useless for job seekers.
choices.D: Only people with four-year degrees benefit from training.

questions.correctAnswerText: Removing small financial barriers can help some adults complete training they are already motivated to pursue.
questions.explanation: The argument depends on the idea that microgrants can make a meaningful difference for motivated learners.

questions.prompt:
Read the literary excerpt and answer the question.
The Window Seat
Amina had taken the night train twice before, but never alone and never with a suitcase that held everything she needed for her first week at the dental assistant program. Her aunt had packed sandwiches, safety pins, and a small flashlight “just in case,” though Amina could not imagine what kind of emergency required all three.
Her ticket placed her by the window. Across the aisle, a man in a work jacket slept with his arms folded over a lunch cooler. A college student highlighted a textbook with fierce attention. Amina opened her own course packet, but the words blurred after the first page. She watched the station lights slide past instead.
At the third stop, an older woman boarded carrying a plant wrapped in newspaper. The only open seat was beside Amina. The woman settled in, noticed the packet, and asked, “First class?”
“First program,” Amina said.
The woman smiled. “Then the first page is always the hardest. After that, pages learn your name.”
Amina laughed because she did not understand the sentence and somehow did. She returned to the packet. This time she underlined the list of required supplies, circled the date of orientation, and wrote one question in the margin.
By dawn, the plant’s leaves had unfolded slightly from the paper. Amina’s suitcase still looked too large, and the program still felt bigger than she was. But the packet was no longer unopened, and the train was still moving.
What is the main conflict in the excerpt?

choices.A: Amina struggles with nervousness as she begins a new training program.
choices.B: Amina tries to convince the conductor to stop the train.
choices.C: Amina loses her suitcase before reaching the station.
choices.D: Amina argues with a college student about a textbook.

questions.correctAnswerText: Amina struggles with nervousness as she begins a new training program.
questions.explanation: The excerpt focuses on Amina’s anxiety and her first steps toward engaging with the program.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Window Seat.”

choices.A: She feels overwhelmed by the importance of starting the program.
choices.B: The packet is written in a language she cannot read.
choices.C: The train has no lights.
choices.D: The older woman takes the packet away.

questions.correctAnswerText: She feels overwhelmed by the importance of starting the program.
questions.explanation: The blurred words show Amina’s nervousness, not a problem with the packet itself.

questions.prompt:

choices.A: It helps Amina feel less intimidated and begin engaging with the packet.
choices.B: It convinces Amina to leave the train at the next stop.
choices.C: It makes Amina throw away her course materials.
choices.D: It causes Amina to stop speaking to other passengers.

questions.correctAnswerText: It helps Amina feel less intimidated and begin engaging with the packet.
questions.explanation: After the older woman’s comment, Amina begins underlining, circling, and writing a question.

choices.A: She underlines the list of required supplies.
choices.B: She circles the date of orientation.
choices.C: She watches station lights slide past.
choices.D: She notices the man with the lunch cooler.
choices.E: She keeps the course packet closed for the entire trip.

questions.correctAnswerText: She underlines the list of required supplies. | She circles the date of orientation.
questions.explanation: These details show Amina beginning to take practical steps.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Window Seat.”
What does the final sentence suggest?

choices.A: Amina is still nervous, but she has begun moving forward.
choices.B: Amina has decided not to attend the program.
choices.C: The train has stopped before reaching its destination.
choices.D: The older woman’s plant has become the main problem.

questions.correctAnswerText: Amina is still nervous, but she has begun moving forward.
questions.explanation: The final sentence shows that the challenge remains, but Amina has started.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “The Window Seat.”
Match each target with the correct tile.

tiles.A: Amina watches station lights instead of reading the packet.
tiles.B: The older woman says the first page is always the hardest.
tiles.C: Amina writes one question in the margin.
tiles.D: The conductor asks Amina to leave the train.

targets.T3: Evidence that Amina starts participating in her own preparation

questions.correctAnswerText: T1=Amina watches station lights instead of reading the packet.; T2=The older woman says the first page is always the hardest.; T3=Amina writes one question in the margin.
questions.explanation: The tiles show hesitation, encouragement, and Amina’s first active step.

questions.prompt:
Read the informational passage and answer the question.
Understanding Warranties
A warranty is a promise that a product or service will meet certain standards for a certain period of time. When a phone, appliance, or tool stops working, a warranty may help a customer get a repair, replacement, or refund. However, warranties differ, and reading the terms before a problem occurs can prevent frustration later.
Some warranties cover only defects in materials or workmanship. This means the company may repair a product that fails because a part was poorly made, but it may not cover damage caused by dropping the item, using it incorrectly, or failing to maintain it. Other warranties are limited by time, parts, labor, or location. A product may have one year of parts coverage but only ninety days of labor coverage.
Customers should also understand what proof is required. Many companies ask for a receipt, serial number, registration record, or photo of the problem. Waiting until the product fails may make these details harder to find. Keeping receipts in a folder or taking a picture of the receipt can make a claim easier.
A warranty is useful, but it is not the same as insurance or a guarantee that nothing will go wrong. It is a written agreement with conditions. A customer who understands those conditions can ask better questions, compare products more wisely, and avoid assuming that every problem will be covered.
What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Understanding warranty terms helps customers know what is covered and what proof may be needed.
choices.B: A warranty guarantees that a product will never break.
choices.C: Customers should throw away receipts after buying expensive products.
choices.D: All warranties cover damage caused by dropping or misusing a product.

questions.correctAnswerText: Understanding warranty terms helps customers know what is covered and what proof may be needed.
questions.explanation: The passage explains warranty coverage limits and the importance of keeping proof.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding Warranties.”
What does a warranty that covers defects in materials or workmanship usually include?

choices.A: Problems caused by poorly made parts or construction
choices.B: Damage caused by dropping the product in water
choices.C: Every possible problem for the lifetime of the customer
choices.D: Replacement of the product without any proof of purchase

questions.correctAnswerText: Problems caused by poorly made parts or construction
questions.explanation: Defects in materials or workmanship refer to problems caused by how the product was made.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding Warranties.”
Why does the passage recommend keeping receipts?

choices.A: Companies may require proof of purchase when a customer makes a warranty claim.
choices.B: Receipts always increase the length of the warranty.
choices.C: Receipts prevent products from breaking.
choices.D: Companies require customers to mail receipts every month.

questions.correctAnswerText: Companies may require proof of purchase when a customer makes a warranty claim.
questions.explanation: The passage says many companies ask for a receipt or similar proof.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding Warranties.”
Which sentence best supports the idea that warranties have limits?

choices.A: “A warranty is a promise that a product or service will meet certain standards for a certain period of time.”
choices.B: “Some warranties cover only defects in materials or workmanship.”
choices.C: “Keeping receipts in a folder or taking a picture of the receipt can make a claim easier.”
choices.D: “A customer who understands those conditions can ask better questions.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Some warranties cover only defects in materials or workmanship.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly shows that warranty coverage may be limited.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Understanding Warranties.”
How is the passage mainly organized?

choices.A: It defines warranties, explains common limits, and gives advice for using them effectively.
choices.B: It tells a story about one customer returning a phone.
choices.C: It ranks products by price from cheapest to most expensive.
choices.D: It argues that customers should never buy products with warranties.

questions.correctAnswerText: It defines warranties, explains common limits, and gives advice for using them effectively.
questions.explanation: The passage defines warranties, describes coverage limits, and recommends keeping proof.

questions.prompt:
Read the editing passage and choose the best words for the blanks.
Tool Checkout Table
(1) The Spring Cleanup Day will include a tool checkout table near the park entrance. (2) Volunteers may borrow gloves, trash grabbers, buckets, and safety vests for the morning shift. (3) Each borrowed item must be returned before volunteers leave the park. (4) [D1], the cleanup team can count supplies and prepare for the afternoon shift. (5) The table [D2] staffed by two volunteers from 8:00 a.m. until noon. (6) Organizers ask participants to report broken tools instead of placing them back on the table.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=In this way; D2=will be
questions.explanation: “In this way” shows how returning items helps the team count supplies, and “will be staffed” is correct.

questions.prompt:
Refer to “Tool Checkout Table.”
Which revision of sentence 6 is most concise while keeping the meaning?

choices.A: Organizers ask participants to report broken tools instead of placing them back on the table.
choices.B: Organizers are asking participants to make reports about broken tools rather than putting broken tools back on the table again.
choices.C: Participants are being asked by organizers to report tools that are broken in condition.
choices.D: Organizers ask, with a request, that participants make a report of broken tools.

questions.correctAnswerText: Organizers ask participants to report broken tools instead of placing them back on the table.
questions.explanation: This sentence is concise and keeps the original meaning.

choices.A: The buckets near the entrance needs to be counted.

choices.D: The box of gloves have been moved to the table.

questions.explanation: The plural subject “workers” correctly takes the plural verb “wear.”

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the clearest pronoun reference?

choices.B: Talia handed the checklist to Morgan, and Morgan signed the checklist.
choices.C: She signed it when Talia handed the checklist to Morgan.
choices.D: When it was handed to Morgan by Talia, she signed it.

questions.correctAnswerText: Talia handed the checklist to Morgan, and Morgan signed the checklist.
questions.explanation: Repeating the nouns makes clear who signed what.

questions.prompt:

The training session was scheduled to end at 4:00. [D1], several questions from new volunteers extended it by twenty minutes.
questions.correctAnswerText: D1=However
questions.explanation: “However” correctly signals contrast between the scheduled ending time and the delay.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?

choices.A: If the gate is locked call the site supervisor.
choices.B: If the gate is locked, call the site supervisor.
choices.C: If, the gate is locked call the site supervisor.
choices.D: If the gate, is locked call the site supervisor.

questions.explanation: The introductory dependent clause should be followed by a comma.

questions.prompt:
Which revision best combines the sentences?
The crew filled ten bags. The bags contained leaves and branches.

choices.A: The crew filled ten bags containing leaves and branches.
choices.B: The bags filled the crew and contained leaves and branches.
choices.C: Containing the crew, ten bags filled leaves and branches.
choices.D: The crew filled, and leaves contained ten bags of branches.

choices.B: The supervisor checks the supply list and ordered more gloves yesterday.
choices.C: The supervisor checked the supply list and ordered more gloves yesterday.
choices.D: The supervisor had checked the supply list and orders more gloves yesterday.

choices.B: Luis carried the sign wearing a reflective vest.
choices.C: Wearing a reflective vest, Luis carried the sign.

questions.prompt:
Read the paired passages and answer the question.
Passage A: Open the School Gym on Weekends
The school district should open the high school gym to residents on Saturday mornings. Many families live in apartments or neighborhoods without safe recreation space. A supervised open gym would give adults and teens a place to walk, play basketball, and participate in simple fitness activities during cold or rainy weather.
The building already belongs to the community, and it sits unused most Saturday mornings. The district could start with a limited schedule, require sign-in, and hire one staff member to supervise. Local health organizations may be willing to provide volunteers or small grants because regular movement supports public health.
Some people worry about damage to school property. That concern is reasonable, but it can be managed with clear rules, restricted access to locker rooms and classrooms, and supervision. Schools are important educational spaces, but they can also serve broader community needs when policies are careful. Opening the gym a few hours each week would be a practical way to use an existing public resource.
Passage B: Keep the Gym Reserved for School Use
The high school gym should remain reserved for school teams, classes, and approved student activities. Opening it to the public on weekends may sound simple, but the costs and responsibilities would add up. The district would need trained supervisors, security procedures, cleaning staff, insurance review, and a system for handling injuries or conflicts.
The gym is also not as unused as it appears. Coaches often schedule practices, equipment checks, and tournament preparation on weekends. Custodians use empty hours to clean floors, inspect bleachers, and prepare the building for Monday. Public use could interfere with these tasks or create wear on equipment that students need.
Community recreation is important, but the school district should not become the main provider of weekend fitness space. The city parks department, recreation center, and local nonprofits are better suited to manage public programs. The district can support community health by sharing information about those programs without taking on a responsibility that may distract from its primary mission.
What is the central difference between the two passages?

choices.A: Passage A supports weekend community use of the gym, while Passage B argues the gym should remain focused on school needs.
choices.B: Passage A argues that students should stop using the gym, while Passage B argues that gyms should be removed from schools.
choices.C: Passage A focuses on cafeteria food, while Passage B focuses on school buses.
choices.D: Passage A supports closing recreation centers, while Passage B supports canceling all student activities.

questions.correctAnswerText: Passage A supports weekend community use of the gym, while Passage B argues the gym should remain focused on school needs.
questions.explanation: The passages disagree about whether the gym should serve broader community recreation.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the school gym.
Which evidence most directly supports Passage A’s argument?

choices.A: The gym sits unused most Saturday mornings and could be opened with limited hours and supervision.
choices.B: Coaches sometimes schedule weekend practices.
choices.C: The district would need insurance review.
choices.D: Custodians prepare the building for Monday.

questions.correctAnswerText: The gym sits unused most Saturday mornings and could be opened with limited hours and supervision.
questions.explanation: This evidence supports Passage A’s claim that the gym could serve the community without major changes.

questions.prompt:
Select TWO answers.
Refer to the paired passages about the school gym.
Which TWO claims from Passage B are supported with reasons or evidence?

choices.A: Weekend public use would require supervision, security procedures, cleaning, and injury planning.
choices.B: Weekend access could interfere with practices, equipment checks, or custodial work.
choices.C: No residents would ever use the gym on weekends.
choices.D: Students do not need the gym for school activities.
choices.E: The parks department is legally forbidden from offering recreation programs.

questions.correctAnswerText: Weekend public use would require supervision, security procedures, cleaning, and injury planning. | Weekend access could interfere with practices, equipment checks, or custodial work.
questions.explanation: Passage B gives these as reasons for keeping the gym reserved for school use.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the school gym.
Which statement from Passage A directly addresses a concern raised by opponents?

choices.A: “Many families live in apartments or neighborhoods without safe recreation space.”
choices.B: “The building already belongs to the community.”
choices.C: “Some people worry about damage to school property.”
choices.D: “Opening the gym a few hours each week would be a practical way to use an existing public resource.”

questions.correctAnswerText: “Some people worry about damage to school property.”
questions.explanation: This sentence directly acknowledges a concern raised by opponents of weekend public use.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the school gym.
Which statement is a supported inference from both passages?

choices.A: Both authors recognize that community recreation has value, but they disagree about whether the school gym should provide it.
choices.B: Both authors believe the gym should be permanently closed.
choices.C: Both authors argue that schools should stop holding student activities.
choices.D: Both authors believe supervision is unnecessary.

questions.correctAnswerText: Both authors recognize that community recreation has value, but they disagree about whether the school gym should provide it.
questions.explanation: Passage A supports gym access, while Passage B suggests other organizations are better suited.

questions.prompt:
Refer to the paired passages about the school gym.
Which additional evidence would most help evaluate both arguments?

choices.A: Data on weekend gym availability, estimated supervision costs, and community demand for indoor recreation
choices.B: A list of school mascot designs from the past decade
choices.C: A survey asking students their favorite lunch menu
choices.D: A history of basketball rules in the United States

questions.correctAnswerText: Data on weekend gym availability, estimated supervision costs, and community demand for indoor recreation
questions.explanation: This evidence would help evaluate both the need for access and the practicality of providing it.

questions.prompt:
Which revision fixes the misplaced modifier?
Covered in warning labels, Keisha carried the paint cans to the storage room.

choices.A: Covered in warning labels, Keisha carried the paint cans to the storage room.
choices.B: Keisha carried the paint cans covered in warning labels to the storage room.
choices.C: Keisha, covered in warning labels, carried the paint cans to the storage room.
choices.D: The storage room carried the paint cans covered in warning labels.

questions.correctAnswerText: Keisha carried the paint cans covered in warning labels to the storage room.
questions.explanation: This revision places the modifier next to “paint cans,” the items covered in warning labels.

questions.prompt:
Which choice best combines the sentences without changing their meaning?
The office installed a water station. The station fills reusable bottles.

choices.A: The office installed a water station that fills reusable bottles.
choices.B: The water station installed the office and fills reusable bottles.
choices.C: Filling the office, reusable bottles installed a water station.
choices.D: The office installed, and the station reusable bottles fills.

questions.correctAnswerText: The office installed a water station that fills reusable bottles.
questions.explanation: This sentence clearly combines the two ideas without changing the meaning.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses commas correctly?

choices.A: The safety manual, which was revised last month, explains the storage rules.
choices.B: The safety manual which was revised, last month explains the storage rules.
choices.C: The safety manual, which was revised last month explains, the storage rules.
choices.D: The safety manual which, was revised last month, explains the storage rules.

questions.correctAnswerText: The safety manual, which was revised last month, explains the storage rules.
questions.explanation: The nonessential clause “which was revised last month” is correctly set off by commas.

questions.prompt:
Read both passages. Write an evidence-based essay of 300–500 words explaining which position is better supported. Use evidence from both passages. Maintain a formal tone and organize your response clearly.
Passage A:
Require Pay Ranges in Job Postings
Employers should be required to include pay ranges in job postings. Applicants spend time preparing resumes, completing forms, and attending interviews. They should not have to wait until the end of the process to learn that a position pays less than they can accept. A clear pay range helps job seekers decide whether to apply and allows them to compare opportunities more fairly.
Pay transparency can also improve trust. When wages are hidden, applicants may wonder whether pay is based on skill or on who negotiates most aggressively. Posting a range does not require every employee to earn the same amount. It simply gives applicants a realistic starting point. Employers can still explain that final pay depends on experience, certification, shift, or location.
Businesses may worry that posting pay ranges will make hiring more difficult. However, unclear postings can waste time for both sides. A manager may review applications, schedule interviews, and contact references only to lose a strong candidate when the pay is finally revealed. Including pay information early can attract applicants who are more likely to accept the job.
A pay-range requirement would not solve every workplace problem, but it would make the hiring process clearer and more respectful. Job seekers need accurate information before making important decisions about their time, transportation, childcare, and finances.
Passage B:
Do Not Require Pay Ranges in Every Posting
Employers should not be required to include pay ranges in every job posting. Pay can depend on many factors, including experience, training, shift availability, location, and business conditions. A posted range may be too broad to be useful or too narrow to reflect what the employer can offer for an unusually strong candidate. Requiring a range may create confusion rather than clarity.
Small businesses may face the greatest difficulty. A large company may have formal salary bands, but a small business owner may adjust pay based on changing revenue, seasonal demand, or the combination of duties a new hire can perform. If the posted range later changes, applicants may feel misled even when the employer acted in good faith.
There are other ways to make hiring fairer. Employers can discuss pay during the first interview, provide written offers before asking candidates to make commitments, and train managers to answer pay questions directly. Job seekers should receive pay information early, but a legal requirement for every posting may be too rigid. Flexible guidelines would encourage transparency without forcing all employers into the same system.
Writing Task:
Which passage presents the better-supported argument about whether employers should be required to include pay ranges in job postings? Write an essay explaining your position. Use evidence from both passages to support your analysis.
Rubric Guidance:
• Analyze which argument is better supported.
• Use relevant evidence from both passages.
• Explain why the evidence supports your claim.
• Address weaknesses or limitations in the less-supported argument.
• Organize ideas logically.
• Use clear sentence structure, grammar, and formal language.
